title_content.content: STRATEGY & ASSET ALLOCATION & PERFORMANCE OF HIGH CONVICTION IDEAS

paragraph_content.content: Since September 2020, we have strongly and repeatedly argued that a classic paradigm shift into the new market leaders was starting, which would feature commodities, commodity-related and inflation-sensitive sectors, and many of the long-depressed markets.
In October 2020, with UST 10-year yields at 0.78%, we warned that bond yields would rise significantly and for a long time as the 40-year bull market in bonds transitions to a secular bear market.

For the past 3-4 years, the great question among Western investors has been: “Is China uninvestable?” As long-term contrarians, that is music to our ears! We believe Chinese stocks are in the early stages of a secular bull market.
Other important recent developments that support the basis for our high conviction themes:

item_content.content:  The ratio of gold to the U.S. Consumer Price Index (CPI) broke-out in September 2024 from a 45-year downtrend-line.
 The outperformance of the gold miners versus the major U.S. stock market indices has broken-out, as suggested by rising bond-market inflation expectations.
 Sensitive inflation indicators in the markets are starting to come alive.
 The U.S. Dollar appears to be entering a long-term downtrend, which is bullish for commodities and inflation.
Silver has outperformed gold. And the relative-performance of the silver miners has broken-out against almost every broad stock-market index in the world.
Agricultural commodities and related-stocks are starting to rise, as several agriculture ETFs and fertilizer stocks have broken-out.

paragraph_content.content: These developments lead to the following conclusions:

item_content.content: 1. Gold is set to outperform the S&P 500 and international stock-markets for many years to come.
2. The shift toward hard assets is likely to gain further momentum.
3. China’s stock-market could be among the best-performing equity markets in the world.

title_content.content: The curse of second order consequences: Six 1 weeks of war has elevated the importance of the Strait of Hormuz in Iran’s geostrategic calculations. Washington thinks the regime is using Hormuz as a tactical negotiating tool. This is a false assumption. Instead, Iran views Hormuz as strategic leverage that will strengthen its relative power in the post-war regional architecture. What are the implications? How to invest.

paragraph_content.content: As we go to press, the ceasefire agreed to on Tuesday evening Eastern time is under growing strain. The U.S. continues to send military assets to the region, the most visible being the USS Bush Carrier Strike Group. And on Wednesday evening, the speaker of the Iranian Parliament, and one of Tehran’s most senior officials, tweeted:
3 clauses [of Iran’s 10-Point Proposal that Trump said would form a workable basis on which to negotiate] have been violated…in such a situation a bilateral ceasefire or negotiations is unreasonable.

However, regardless of whether the ceasefire holds, there is a deeper and far more consequential development that could reshape the regional balance of power in the Middle East and by extension have broader global implications.

Six weeks of war appears to have re-prioritized and elevated the Strait of Hormuz in Tehran’s geostrategic calculus. Tehran now views Hormuz as critical to restoring its deterrence, to ensuring its national security and to reshaping the regional post-war order in its favor. This is a big shift from six weeks ago when the regime’s nuclear programme, network of regional proxies

page_footer_content.content: 13D RESEARCH & STRATEGY, 13D.COM | PRINT ONCE - DO NOT FORWARD - DO NOT COPY

paragraph_content.content: April 9, 2026

and ballistic missile programme were its primary modes of deterrence. From the regime’s perspective, this hybrid approach failed to provide sufficient deterrence to prevent the U.S. and Israel from assassinating the Supreme Leader and initiating six weeks of strikes on military and civilian infrastructure.

A new and evolving deterrent strategy now appears to be in the works whereby Tehran is looking to place heavier emphasis on economic deterrence going forwards alongside its current military-deterrence capabilities. It is within this context that the regime is starting to leverage what it describes as “an imposed war”
( \cos \frac { \cos } { \cdot } \sin \alpha \cos \beta \cos \alpha )

by American and Israeli aggressors to fundamentally reframe the role of the Strait of Hormuz in its geostrategic calculus such that Tehran emerges as an integral and elevated actor in the post-war regional architecture.

The last six weeks have underlined to Tehran how its most powerful weapon is not so much its missile and drone capabilities per se but rather its ability to de facto control the flow of vessels through the Strait of Hormuz and by extension the leverage this creates on global energy markets, insurance rates and maritime security.

This evolution in Tehran’s deterrence strategy was on full display last week when the Iranian Parliament initiated a fast-track process to formalise into law Tehran’s management of the Strait of Hormuz. According to one Iranian MP, the plan has four objectives: shipping security, environmental pollution tolls, collecting fees for navigational guidance and creating a regional development fund for reconstruction. Underscoring this shift, the Iranian navy earlier this week said that the Strait of Hormuz would “never return to its former state” and vowed to impose a “new Gulf order.” And last weekend, in an early sign of coordination between Tehran and Oman – the other coastal state on the Strait of Hormuz – Iranian and Omani officials met to discuss “possible options for ensuring smooth passage through the Strait of Hormuz.”

Six weeks ago, around 20% of global energy flows – not to mention a significant share of global petrochemicals, ammonia, urea, sulphur and helium – flowed through Hormuz unimpeded. However, if Tehran is now repositioning Hormuz as central to its national security and deterrence posture, with vessels needing to coordinate with the IRGC to ensure safe passage, then that has potentially seismic and longer-lasting implications.

page_footer_content.content: 13D RESEARCH & STRATEGY, 13D.COM | PRINT ONCE - DO NOT FORWARD - DO NOT COPY

paragraph_content.content: April 9, 2026

First, it affords Tehran enormous influence and power to effectively run a permissioned gate-keeper toll system through the Strait of Hormuz. And here lies a critical nuance. For the regime, a complete closure of Hormuz is not a sustainable strategy as it would risk further isolation and would invite overwhelming international pressure. However, a permissioned Strait of Hormuz – one that remains open for non-combatant countries and those not allied with the U.S./Israel – is a very different proposition. Pursuing this path, Tehran will calculate that it has time on its side until countries faced with growing economic pressure to either export their products (i.e. the Gulf countries) or to secure supplies (i.e. Asia/Africa/potentially some European countries) approach Tehran on a one-to-one basis to secure a deal and guarantee safe passage.

While vessel traffic through Hormuz is still a fraction of what it was before February 28, a growing list of countries including India, China, Russia, Iraq and a number of South East Asian countries have managed to secure safe passage for some of their vessels by engaging diplomatically with Iran. This in itself has had the unintended consequence of bolstering the international recognition of the regime at a time when it was weakened following the January protests and now, after six weeks of war, has become even more radical, embittered and revengeful towards U.S./Israeli interests. With Trump’s stinging rebukes of NATO’s non-involvement in the war coupled with Foreign Policy last week describing U.S.-South Korea relations as being at “breaking point,” it begs the question “who has become more isolated in the last six weeks: US/Israel, or Iran?”

An article this week in the New York Times by Robert Pape, Professor of political science at the University of Chicago, argued how Iran’s permissioned system over the Strait of Hormuz could elevate its relative power. In a piece titled “The War Is Turning Iran Into a Major World Power,” Pape argued:

In recent years, the conventional geopolitical wisdom has been that the world order was moving toward three centers of power: the United States, China and Russia. That view assumed that power derived primarily from economic scale and military capability.

That assumption no longer holds. A fourth center of global power is quickly emerging — Iran — that does not rival those three nations economically or militarily. Instead, its newfound power derives from its control over the most important energy choke point in the global economy, the Strait of Hormuz.

page_footer_content.content: 13D RESEARCH & STRATEGY, 13D.COM | PRINT ONCE - DO NOT FORWARD - DO NOT COPY

paragraph_content.content: April 9, 2026
President Macron of France echoed similar comments last week when he said:
The liberation of the Strait of Hormuz by force through a military operation… It is unrealistic…This can only be done in concert with Iran.
However, Hormuz taking a more central role in Tehran’s national security and deterrence posture has implications beyond geopolitics. It also has global geoeconomic implications.
Just over four years ago, in WILTW March 3, 2022, we discussed the importance that the so-called Triple Axis of China, Russia and Iran would have on the global order.
We wrote:

While the relationship between the three countries is nuanced, and internal differences and suspicions do exist, they are nonetheless bound by a common cause of achieving self-reliance, reducing their dependence on the dollar and challenging the incumbent international order that, from their perspective, they had no say in creating.  These motivations will only grow with time. They will not dissipate.
We went onto quote from a book called Triple Axis: Iran’s Relations with Russia and China, by Dina Esfandiary and Ariane Tabatabai:

The three countries seek to assert themselves as regional hegemons and reduce the influence of the West, particularly the United States, in what they view as their own backyards. Ultimately, Beijing, Moscow and Tehran want the international order to better reflect the interests of “rising” non-Western states…

Fast forward to today and Iran’s nascent efforts to formalise its leverage over Hormuz aligns with Moscow and Beijing’s longer-term ambitions to challenge the dollar’s monopoly on energy pricing and to create an alternative monetary, trade and financial system that is based on gold as the reserve asset and not U.S. Treasuries. While Iran is sanctioned out of the Western financial system, receiving payments in CNY to allow vessels through Hormuz could further boost Beijing’s efforts to internationalise the CNY and to financialise the currency around gold (see reports).

page_footer_content.content: 13D RESEARCH & STRATEGY, 13D.COM | PRINT ONCE - DO NOT FORWARD - DO NOT COPY

paragraph_content.content: April 9, 2026

Seen from this light, Hormuz is not simply a tactical tool to be negotiated by Iran to secure a temporary ceasefire (which as we have argued in WILTWs March 12 and March 19 is not what the regime wants). Rather, Hormuz now represents something far consequential; it represents a critical jigsaw piece in a broader strategic game that seeks to reorder the security architecture around global energy flows and the monetary system that underpins it.

The cleanest way for investors to leverage these developments is through gold bullion, the 13D Gold Miners Index and the 13D Oil & Gas Index. We suspect markets will remain headline-driven and volatile over the coming weeks. We have raised 21.28% in 1-3 Month T-Bills to take advantage of further opportunities that will likely present themselves over the coming weeks and months.

title_content.content: 2 One year after “Liberation Day,” the combination of tariff costs, trade-policy uncertainty and supply-chain disruptions is likely to undermine America’s ability to service its debt while meeting increasing defense and entitlement spending. Slowing GDP growth will negatively impact tax revenues while inflation erodes consumer purchasing power. This is why the recent correction in gold is likely to prove shortlived.

paragraph_content.content: We have followed the performance of key economic trends in the trade-policy debate since tariffs were announced in April 2025, including trade deficits, manufacturing investment and employment, inbound FDI and price inflation.

page_footer_content.content: 13D RESEARCH & STRATEGY, 13D.COM | PRINT ONCE - DO NOT FORWARD - DO NOT COPY

paragraph_content.content: April 9, 2026

(See WILTWs April 3, April 17, June 5, July 31 and November 27, 2025.) Adding to the mountain of evidence, the Cato Institute’s Scott Lincicome, Alfredo Obregon and Chad Smitson published a summary of key tariff effects in an April 2nd article titled “One Year After Liberation Day: Here’s What We Know and What We Don’t.” Below, we discuss some of their conclusions as well as ours.

Price inflation. In our view, any discussion of the effects of tariffs must focus first and foremost on price impacts, diverted corporate profits and the loss of purchasing power. As we have noted previously, the tariffs exerted enough upward pressure on overall price levels for goods that they broke their long-term deflationary trend. (See left-hand chart below.)

However, it is also true that U.S. importers absorbed most of the cost of the tariffs early-on, although this trend appears to have diminished in recent months. Furthermore, U.S. businesses accelerated their build-up of inventories in advance of the tariffs, providing a boost to imports early in the year. (See right-hand chart below.)

paragraph_content.content: Subsequent to Liberation Day, the growth in U.S. business inventories decelerated sharply, with the business-inventory-to-sales ratio finishing January at the lowest level in 3.5 years. It appears that businesses have been rebuilding their inventories more recently, after the IEEPA tariffs were invalidated by the Supreme Court.

page_footer_content.content: 13D RESEARCH & STRATEGY, 13D.COM | PRINT ONCE - DO NOT FORWARD - DO NOT COPY

paragraph_content.content: April 9, 2026

The inflationary effect of the tariffs is likely to hit harder over the course of 2026, and this will be exacerbated by the Persian Gulf supply-chain disruptions for crude oil, petrochemicals, fertilizers, aluminum and other commodities. YTD price changes exceed 30% for many commodities, and the U.S. is indeed bearing the brunt of those increases even though it imports very little crude oil (less than 1 mb/d) directly from the Gulf. (See charts below.) As Dartmouth economist Douglas Irwin has noted, more than half of U.S. imports are used as inputs for domestically-manufactured goods.

paragraph_content.content: Trade deficits. One of Washington’s main goals was the reduction of the trade deficit in goods. But trade deficits did not fall as hoped, partly due to the frontloading of inventories and also due to accelerated imports of non-monetary gold after initial fears of tariffs on the yellow metal circulated. Excluding gold imports, there has been little change in the trade deficit since April 2025. (See charts below.)

paragraph_content.content: Inbound FDI. Although the White House frequently advertises trillions of dollars in promises of investment from other countries, so far, foreign direct investment (FDI) inflows have not registered any growth. In fact, inbound FDI has been in a downward trend since 2Q-2025, and total FDI inflows of $288.4 billion in 2025 were lower than the annual totals from 2021 to 2024, according to Cato. (See chart below.)

This rate of FDI inflows is far short of the amount needed to achieve the administration’s stated goal of $18 trillion of foreign investment, although this figure has been widely criticized for being over-inflated and non-binding. And given the current economic and geopolitical strains over the situation in Iran, the likelihood of meaningful near-term increases in foreign investment appears slim. With Persian Gulf sovereign wealth funds (SWFs) controlling $5 trillion of assets, and the EU holding investments of $12.6 trillion in the U.S., the risk of repatriation of capital flows has never been greater.

paragraph_content.content: Trade-policy uncertainty. Uncertainty for business planners has been exacerbated by the haphazard way the tariffs were implemented and also due to the challenges of getting refunds of IEEPA tariffs that were invalidated by the Supreme Court. (See charts below.) For many small companies, tariff refunds can be the difference between staying in business or not. And, we suspect that corporate hiring and capex decisions will increasingly be put on hold the longer the risk of renewed hostilities in Iran lingers.

paragraph_content.content: U.S. trade partners are making their own deals among themselves. It is no surprise that America’s traditional trade partners are increasingly signing deals among themselves (see chart below) given the White House’s hostility toward global trade and its loss of trust among U.S. allies. (See WILTWs April 3 and July 17, 2025.)

This upends decades of trade liberalization that enabled real exports of goods and services to grow 4.9% annually in the U.S. over the past forty years, nearly double the rate of real GDP growth. Exports of goods and services in nominal terms grew from nearly 7% of GDP in 1985 to around 11% in 2025, after topping-out above 13% in the early-2010s.

The bitter irony of “America First” is that U.S. consumers will be saddled with higher inflation, lower purchasing power and slower job creation as other countries reap the economic benefits of reduced trade barriers while increasing their global competitiveness.

paragraph_content.content: Manufacturing jobs and facilities. Manufacturing employment and spending on construction of manufacturing facilities have both fallen over the past 12 to 18 months—the opposite of what was intended. Real private fixed investment in manufacturing structures fell 16% between 3Q-2024 and 4Q-2025, while the number of employees in manufacturing has fallen over 100,000 since early last year. (See charts below.) Although these trends may stabilize in the near future, we doubt they will reverse meaningfully and sustainably given the supply-chain disruptions in the Middle East.

paragraph_content.content: The aforementioned trends will undermine the economic growth that is needed to generate future tax revenues, fund social insurance programs and defense spending, and service the growing debt load. title_content.content: 3 The Escalation Gap: why U.S. military conventional choices are limited, and could trap it into crossing the nuclear threshold.

paragraph_content.content: The U.S. military maintains a potent expeditionary force, and, in theory, can scale up to project impressive conventional-military power anywhere in the world before needing to resort to nuclear weapons. However, for a variety of practical and political reasons, the U.S. can’t quickly add more conventional military

page_footer_content.content: 13D RESEARCH & STRATEGY, 13D.COM | PRINT ONCE - DO NOT FORWARD - DO NOT COPY

paragraph_content.content: April 9, 2026

pressure after reaching a certain limited level of force, a level it has now reached in the Middle East. This leaves a dangerous gap between the use of limited military force and the need to use nuclear weapons to salvage an impasse such as the conflict in Iran.

Given the global reach of its bomber force; a world-wide network of U.S. Air Force (USAF) bases with shorter range fighter-bombers; by far the world's largest aerial refueling tanker fleet to extend their range; combined with an Army brigadesized Immediate Response Force of around 5,000 airborne troops with transport aircraft that can deploy them on 18 hours notice, the U.S. can rapidly deliver thousands of pounds of bombs and a small-but-effective ground fighting force anywhere in the world within a day. In addition, the U.S. normally has six aircraft-carrier-strike groups (CSG) at sea, each with an air wing of 70 aircraft and three or four missile launching ships and submarine escorts. It also typically maintains three Marine Expeditionary Units (MEU), each with 2,500 Marines along with their own transport, supply and aircraft support who are able to arrive on any shoreline within two weeks. Elite special operations units such as the U.S. Army Rangers and their associated aviation support rounds out this capability with several thousand more specialized soldiers. It is this vanguard that has been brought to bear against Iran.

U.S. military planners designed this force to rapidly bring a considerable amount of firepower against a potential adversary under two general scenarios: a defined operation using air power and up to 30,000 troops or so lasting no more than 4-5 weeks to achieve a specific goal, or as an advance force to “buy time” while units that take longer to assemble are organized and moved into the operational zone. Actions such as Operation Absolute Resolve (the capture of Venezuelan President Maduro) are examples of the first type of operation, whereas the initial actions in Afghanistan shortly after the 9/11 attacks are an example of the second. In brief, for both budget and operational reasons, the U.S. military is not designed to be able to immediately undertake sustained actions requiring a large standing military.

It takes time to mobilize and deploy major active-duty units, much less the Reserve or National Guard units they need for additional logistical and combat support. Operation Desert Shield and the subsequent Operation Desert Storm against Iraq are good examples. An alert brigade of the 82nd Airborne was on the ground for Desert Shield only 31 hours after the initial orders, and additional

page_footer_content.content: 13D RESEARCH & STRATEGY, 13D.COM | PRINT ONCE - DO NOT FORWARD - DO NOT COPY

paragraph_content.content: April 9, 2026

air defense and USAF units followed within days. However, it took six months to gather the 500,000 troops needed for Desert Storm, the actual assault to overcome the Iraqi occupation of Kuwait. A similar period of time was required prior to the final invasion of Iraq in 2003.

Given the smaller size of today’s armed forces, it would be difficult to assemble a force that size to invade Iran even if the U.S. wanted to do so. In addition, political factors are also stacked against mobilizing a larger force. In both 1991 and 2003, there was broad popular support for the disruption and cost of mobilization. For the U.S. to send significant additional army units to face Iran, much less actually call up reserves, would be unthinkable in today’s political environment, with mid-term elections only six months away, support for the war with Iran under 40% and presidential approval hovering at 35% and even lower in some polls.

We have previously discussed (WILTW March 5th) the limited time frame in which the U.S. can sustain military operations. Therefore, the U.S. faces a near insoluble dilemma. In Iran, it has run out of options beyond one last “shock and awe” conventional bombing campaign. If that failed, given the inability of the U.S. and Israel to neutralize the Iranian missile bases located underground, tactical nuclear weapons would be perhaps the only remaining option to defeat Iran.

Over the last two decades, as the U.S. has drawn down its sizable Cold-War era conventional military, it has developed and deployed several new classes of small, selectable-yield nuclear weapons. These weapons were designed specifically with targets in Iran and North Korea in mind. Both North Korea and Iran have facilities tunneled deep within granite mountains that are nearly impervious to conventional explosives. In contrast, a small nuclear bomb, with its much larger shockwave, could potentially destroy them.

Nuclear weapon yields are measured in thousands of tons of TNT, or kilotons (kt). It is important to realize these new devices are not large, city destroying weapons. These are far smaller than the weapons used in World War II on Hiroshima and Nagasaki, which were approximately 25kt, but are under 5kt and in some cases can be selected to yield 1kt or less. Still, a 1kt nuclear weapon is a hundred times more powerful than the largest non-nuclear weapon in the U.S. arsenal, the GBU-57, which has a yield of about 10 tons of TNT or 0.01 kt. In addition, the heat, radiation, and electromagnetic pulse (EMP) can

page_footer_content.content: 13D RESEARCH & STRATEGY, 13D.COM | PRINT ONCE - DO NOT FORWARD - DO NOT COPY

paragraph_content.content: April 9, 2026
cause damage in ways conventional explosions cannot, assuring the destruction of these highly-protected targets.

Is the administration seriously considering using nuclear weapons? The White House response has been ambiguous, with the rapid response twitter account denying that comments by Vice President Vance implied their potential use. Yet, shortly after, Press Secretary Karoline Leavitt said “Only the President knows where things stand and what he will do.” President Trump has in the past suggested that he would be willing to use them, even prior to his election in 2018. But crossing the nuclear threshold would carry many unpredictable risks, and may well have been a factor in the sudden moves to try to end the Iran war this week.

Most discussion of the use of tactical nuclear weapons has centered on their potential use by Russia, despite the fact that for the reasons noted above the most likely first user would be the United States. Until recently there was little discussion of the ramifications of first use by the U.S. The most likely immediate impact would be widespread international condemnation. Typically, the international community would institute crippling sanctions and trade restrictions, however, while the U.S. role has diminished over recent years, the central role it still plays in the Western economic system would be hard to replace on short notice. Nevertheless, nuclear weapon use by the U.S. would dramatically accelerate the attempts by many countries to disengage from America.

A major result of the use of a nuclear weapon would be a mad scramble by nonnuclear states who have the ability to obtain them to achieve a nuclear capacity as quickly as possible. A number of states are thought to have the capability to rapidly build nuclear weapons, such as Japan, Germany, South Korea, and Saudi Arabia. Other countries could likely do so within years. Essentially anyone currently running a nuclear power plant could technically build a nuclear weapon – and many would probably do so.

For over a decade, military planners have been warning that the U.S. was ill equipped to engage in a sustained war of attrition against even a second-tier power, much less a near-peer adversary such as Russia or China. These warnings have proven prescient as America enters its second month of the campaign against Iran, and faces the exhaustion of its precision conventional arsenal with

page_footer_content.content: 13D RESEARCH & STRATEGY, 13D.COM | PRINT ONCE - DO NOT FORWARD - DO NOT COPY

paragraph_content.content: April 9, 2026

a limited ability to escalate conventionally by bringing in the large ground forces needed for an invasion. This leaves the U.S. with a stark choice: accept defeat, or use the ultimate weapon, with all the ramifications that entails. While the current crisis may or may not be abating, this is a choice the U.S. will face again unless it reforms its conventional military capacity, or charts a different foreign policy path.

title_content.content: This year will be the first since 2021 that China’s annual net-wealth creation will be positive. With China’s consumerdiscretionary stocks at an extreme underweight level, the time could be ripe to buy them. This week, we are adding Midea to the 13D China Cyclical Opportunities and Anti-deflation Index.

paragraph_content.content: The bear market in China’s property sector has reportedly erased some 150 trillion yuan of wealth in the past five years or so, implying an average wealth loss of around 30 trillion yuan per year. Over time, the negative wealth effect has diminished as lower base effects kicked-in: a 10% decline in property prices today implies a wealth loss of 25 trillion yuan, compared to nearly 40 trillion yuan five years ago.

On the other hand, since its bottom in September 2024, the Chinese stock market (now with a total market cap of 110 trillion yuan) is growing at around 15%-20% every year (roughly 20 trillion yuan), in addition to China having the highest saving rate in the world. Furthermore, the real economy is growing at around 5%, adding a total of 25 trillion yuan of new wealth since 2021 (or roughly 7 trillion yuan per year).
Together, the stock market and real economy should have a combined positive wealth effect of roughly 27 trillion yuan this year, overwhelming

page_footer_content.content: 13D RESEARCH & STRATEGY, 13D.COM | PRINT ONCE - DO NOT FORWARD - DO NOT COPY

paragraph_content.content: April 9, 2026

the 25 trillion yuan estimated loss from the housing market. Therefore, even assuming that the bear market in China’s property sector continues, it is highly likely that starting this year, there will be a net positive wealth effect for the first time since 2021.

This will be hugely supportive for household consumption, especially discretionary items such as cars, home appliances, premium liquor, and the like. Corroborating this point, since the beginning of this year, we have seen the fundamentals of discretionary consumption improve. Consider the following:

item_content.content: Kweichow Moutai, China’s premium liquor maker, raised the ex-factory/ wholesale price of its flagship 53-degree Feitian Moutai to CNY 1,269 from CNY 1,169 per 500-ml bottle and increased the retail price from CNY 1,499 to CNY 1,539 per 500-ml bottle, effective March 31st. This is Moutai’s first price hike in 30 months.

L’Oréal’s China market sales growth accelerated from 1% year-over-year in 1H 2025 to 5% year-over-year in 2H 2025. Sales of North Asia—home to the China market—stabilized in 2025, increasing 0.5% year-overyear, reversing a two-year decline. According to CEO Nicolas Hieronimus, premium consumption in the China market is recovering, which has strengthened L’Oréal’s confidence.

The mainland market of Estée Lauder, a premium skincare brand, delivered organic growth of 13% year-over-year, registering the second consecutive quarter (ended December 31, 2025) of double-digit growth and making the Chinese market a key contributor to the group’s overall recovery.
Nationwide, various home-appliance makers are reportedly planning a round of price hikes of about 10%, the first price hike since 2021.
 There also have been sporadic reports on price hikes for cement, a key input for real estate and infrastructure projects.

In December 2025, Midea, together with other major AC makers, signed the Self-Regulatory Convention on Enhanced Aluminum Application, promoting the substitution of aluminum for copper in AC heat exchangers. Replacing copper with aluminum can significantly reduce costs, enabling Midea to gain market share without sacrificing its gross margins.
Midea’s H-shares trade at a P/BV ratio of 2.7x and now carry an implied dividend yield of 5.1%.

Fuyao Glass Industry (600660 CH, CNY 57.68; 3606 HK, HKD 61.25) is both the world’s and China’s largest automotive-glass manufacturer, commanding over 37% of the global market in 2025, up 3 percentage points from 2024.

By leveraging vertical integration—producing 90% of its float glass in-house— Fuyao achieves significant cost advantages over competitors, with a gross margin of 37.3% and a net margin of 20.4% in 2025, far exceeding the industry average, which was below 30% and below 15%, respectively.
Fuyao’s H-shares trades at a P/BV ratio of 3.8x, versus its all-time low P/ BV ratio of 3.5x registered in August 2024. Fuyao’s H-shares now carry an implied dividend yield of 4.8%.

title_content.content: “We urgently need something to lead us out of the shallows back into the depths, out of the two-dimensional world of the abstract and theoretical, into the full-bloodedness of embodied life.” How did we lose touch?

paragraph_content.content: Iain McGilchrist has dedicated his work to understanding the discrepancy between the left and right hemispheres of the brain, and the kinds of worlds that emerge when their relationship becomes imbalanced. His claim is not merely neurological, but civilizational: the crisis we face reflects a deeper shift in how we attend to reality.
This piece marks the beginning of a four-part series examining that shift. Here, we begin with diagnosis: how did we lose reality? In the pieces that follow in

page_footer_content.content: 13D RESEARCH & STRATEGY, 13D.COM | PRINT ONCE - DO NOT FORWARD - DO NOT COPY

paragraph_content.content: April 9, 2026

coming weeks, we will turn to the divided mind and the relationship between mythos and logos, the rise of power without wisdom, the externalization of left-hemisphere modes of thought into systems, and finally the possibility of returning to depth and recovering meaning. This is not simply cultural decline. It reflects a more fundamental transformation in how we encounter the world.

McGilchrist begins with a deceptively simple observation: human beings learn through experience which comes with maturation. Unlike machines, we age, we witness change, and we remember how things can be otherwise. As he writes:

You see more, not of course because of any innately superior faculty, but for no better reason than that you have lived through many changes and remember how things can be different, for better or worse, in a way which is hard to understand unless you experienced it.

Experience, in this sense, is not incidental. It is foundational. It grounds judgment in a way that abstract knowledge cannot. Yet, we are increasingly distanced from it. Not only are opportunities for lived, embodied experience eroding, but even where experience is available, it is often subordinated to theory. In the absence of experience, or in the refusal to trust it, we turn instead to abstraction as our guide. This inversion lies at the core of McGilchrist’s concern: a world in which theory begins to replace life.

This tension is not new. In the Meno, Plato presents the thought experiment known as the road to Larissa, which asks whether experience or theoretical knowledge is the better guide to action. The contrast is clear: one may know the route to Larissa through repeated travel or possess a correct account of it through reasoning. Both may arrive, but only one has lived the path. What is new is the extent to which we now favor the latter.

We are raising a generation for whom experience itself can be substituted. It is increasingly sufficient to know about something without ever undergoing it. One can understand heartbreak as a concept, simulate it through virtual systems, or model its emotional contours without ever having risked attachment or loss.

page_footer_content.content: 13D RESEARCH & STRATEGY, 13D.COM | PRINT ONCE - DO NOT FORWARD - DO NOT COPY

paragraph_content.content: April 9, 2026

But this substitution is not neutral. To know that heartbreak is painful is not the same as having one’s world reorganized by it. The latter is not information, it is transformation. It unfolds in time: in the first spark of connection, the slow accumulation of trust, the quiet weaving together of two lives, and then, just as gradually—or all at once—the rupture. It is the disorientation of absence, the strange persistence of memory, the way a song, a street, a passing scent can reopen what one believed had closed.

No simulation can reproduce this. It cannot replicate the investment of self, the vulnerability of exposure, or the irreversible passage through joy into loss. These are not variables to be modeled, but experiences that reconstitute the person who undergoes them. This is why developmental psychologists emphasize that learning is not merely intellectual. A child does not simply acquire knowledge but develops through embodied interaction with the world through movement, resistance, and failure. Abstract reasoning and higher-order cognition are not prior to this process but emerge from it.
McGilchrist connects this erosion of experience to a broader cultural shift. Citing Walter Benjamin, he writes:

The art of storytelling is coming to an end. It is as if something that seemed inalienable to us, the securest among our possessions, were taken from us: the ability to exchange experiences. One reason for this phenomenon is obvious: experience has fallen in value. And it looks as if it is continuing to fall into bottomlessness.

The contemporary obsession with “storytelling” only confirms this diagnosis. Across industries, companies now compete to hire “storytellers,” as if the capacity to give meaningful form to experience were a scarce technical skill (See WILTW February 26, 2026). But that is precisely the point. What was once a shared human inheritance has become something to be outsourced. The ability to tell stories, draw from lived experience, render it intelligible, and pass it on no longer resides within a culture, but must be artificially reintroduced into it. That we must now hire storytellers is itself evidence that we have forgotten how to tell stories. Author Joan Didion said it best: “We tell ourselves stories in order to live.” The human mind needs to create narratives to extract meaning and make sense of chaos, suffering, joy, gratitude, and so on.

page_footer_content.content: 13D RESEARCH & STRATEGY, 13D.COM | PRINT ONCE - DO NOT FORWARD - DO NOT COPY

paragraph_content.content: April 9, 2026

This is what Benjamin saw with such clarity about a century ago: as experience diminishes, so too does the ability to communicate it. What disappears is not simply a form of expression, but a mode of truth. This is why McGilchrist can insist that “the art of storytelling is vital to truth.” And why its loss leaves us not only less connected, but less able to recognize truth when it appears.

For much of human history, myths were not regarded as falsehoods, but as vessels of insight. We have written at length on the importance of fables and myths, not merely for teaching children, but for highlighting what makes us uniquely human (See WILTW July 13, 2023, May 29, 2025, June 5, 2025). Myths carried patterns of meaning that could not be reduced to conceptual language. As McGilchrist writes: “myths embody wisdom that cannot be conveyed in the vapid language of concepts.”

What emerges, then, is not simply a culture that has changed its habits, but one that has altered its way of seeing. We have not just accumulated new tools or new technologies; we have come to inhabit a different world that is increasingly mediated by abstraction, detached from experience, and flattened into what can be modeled, predicted, and controlled.

This is why the problem cannot be reduced to any single domain. It is not only that our technologies simulate experience, or that our institutions privilege theory over practice, or that storytelling has been hollowed out. It is that the very faculty by which we recognize reality— by which we encounter depth, context, and meaning —has been displaced. To lose touch with reality, in this sense, is not to step outside of it, but to begin mistaking its representations for the thing itself. We have not lost reality all at once, but learned, slowly, to look past it. What remains is to recover the depth we no longer know how to see.

title_content.content: 6 An electromagnetic pulse attack is one of America’s greatest vulnerabilities in infrastructure warfare and could be catastrophic if combined with other asymmetric weapons. What are the implications? How to invest.

paragraph_content.content: Nearly two decades ago, we explored the potential impact that an electromagnetic pulse (EMP) weapon could have on Western economies (see WILTW August 7, 2008, and other reports). Although the risk of a nuclear-based EMP attack is low, it is an existential, high-consequence danger. Of all the possible types of asymmetric attacks, this is the one that scares us the most.

Non-nuclear-based EMP weapons can inflict localized and regionalized devastation of strategic targets, especially when combined with other asymmetric weapons, whether cyber or biological-based (i.e., a compound attack). The risk of an asymmetric attack by Iran or its proxies (or one that is blamed on Iran) on the U.S. homeland grows more likely as the West-Asia conflict persists (see section 1). If the U.S. elects to detonate tactical nuclear or EMP devices in Iran, America would likely quickly become a target for asymmetric attacks. Progress has been made in hardening America’s infrastructure, but significant vulnerabilities remain across critical networks.

The threat of an EMP attack has hovered on the margins of U.S. defense debate for two decades, mostly as a theoretical worst-case. However, the West-Asia conflict has given the underlying logic of EMPs new urgency—not because a high-altitude nuclear detonation is imminent, but because infrastructure warfare is now an active feature of the battlespace.
Consider the following:

An EMP attack can affect every electrical and networked system simultaneously. A nuclear-based EMP attack occurs when a nuclear warhead detonates above Earth’s atmosphere at an altitude between 40 and 400 kilometers. This explosion wouldn’t cause fallout, so there’s no direct

page_footer_content.content: 13D RESEARCH & STRATEGY, 13D.COM | PRINT ONCE - DO NOT FORWARD - DO NOT COPY

paragraph_content.content: April 9, 2026

physiological danger to humans. However, it would emit gamma rays that create Compton electrons, which move nearly at the speed of light along the line of sight, damaging electrical and electronic systems. A weapon detonated at an altitude of 300 miles above Chicago would send an electromagnetic wave covering the entire country. It would also destroy a substantial amount of the satellite infrastructure on which America is highly dependent. The 1962 Starfish Prime test at 400 kilometers caused electrical failures in Hawaii approximately 1,400 kilometers away.

The true magnitude of the EMP threat extends beyond immediate destruction to the cascading consequences that follow. The electric grid serves as the foundation for nearly all other systems—water distribution, fuel supply, transportation, communications, healthcare, and food logistics—and when it fails, these systems begin to break down in rapid succession. Because modern cities rely on just-in-time supply chains and maintain only a few days’ worth of essential resources, even a short-term disruption can escalate into widespread shortages.

If outages last weeks, months, or even years, the inability to restore power— partly due to long replacement times for critical grid parts—could cause major societal disruption. In this scenario, the country’s interconnected, tech-dependent infrastructure becomes vulnerable, as power outages halt the mechanisms sustaining daily life, risking large-scale humanitarian consequences. In a nutshell, America’s infrastructure could theoretically resemble that of the 1830s, which supported a population of about 30 million.

The risk of a localized or regional non-nuclear EMP attack carries higher plausibility for tactical or facility-level strikes. Non-nuclear EMP includes high-power microwave (HPM) weapons, intentional electromagneticinterference (IEMI) devices, and specialized bombs that can couple energy into electronics without a nuclear blast. A cluster of critical assets or infrastructure, including substations, data centers, and industrial control rooms, can be paralyzed.

Major militaries have demonstrated and fielded non-nuclear-based EMP weapons specifically to disable electronics over localized areas. Nonstate actors and terrorists could also potentially obtain or assemble IEMI devices with state support or access to specialized components.

page_footer_content.content: 13D RESEARCH & STRATEGY, 13D.COM | PRINT ONCE - DO NOT FORWARD - DO NOT COPY

paragraph_content.content: April 9, 2026

Iran is believed to have the scientific and industrial knowledge base to pursue non-nuclear EMP weapons, regardless of whether related facilities were damaged in the current conflict. A 2025 West Asia News Agencylinked analysis cited an Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) associated general claiming Iran already had “electromagnetic (EMP) missiles” capable of disabling electronics out to roughly 200 km. A 2025 SpecialEurasia analysis also described how Iran’s new “Rastakhiz” missile is integrating nuclear and EMP-strike capabilities, framing EMP as part of Iran’s deterrent posture.

The U.S. government has several initiatives to mitigate risks, but progress remains insufficient. The U.S. is hardening infrastructure against asymmetric EMP attacks and large solar flares, which can produce similar effects. Initiatives to modernize and harden grid infrastructure from multiple threats, including EMPs, total over $10 billion in funding. These initiatives include the 2019 presidential-executive order on EMP resilience, energysector strategies led by the Department of Energy, and ongoing defense and infrastructure-hardening programs across the Pentagon, Department of Homeland Security, and utilities.

However, huge gaps remain in coverage, standards, funding, and restoration capacity across the civilian grid and other critical sectors. Large power transformers are a key vulnerability. Loss of numerous transformers in a large-scale attack could disable the grid for years. Large power transformers are also difficult to shield, and many substations still use control houses, cables, and grounding schemes that are not designed to withstand EMPs. This exposes digital protective relays, industrial-control systems, and communications cables to induced surges.

The most immediate, and potentially greatest homeland risk from the West-Asia conflict is a prolonged, multifaceted strategy combining traditional terrorist violence with unconventional methods. In the near term, the pathway from regional conflict to U.S. homeland impact is far more likely to take the form of coordinated hybrid interference rather than a single, nuclear-based EMP attack. For instance, combining a non-nuclear EMP weapon with an advanced cyberattack or physical attack on a strategic asset would amplify the effects (see WILTW April 12, 2018).

In this context, hybrid operations represent the most credible and immediate mechanism through which a regional conflict could generate tangible consequences inside the United States.

title_content.content: Two landmark cases aligned social media 7 companies with Big Tobacco—and won. What are the implications?

paragraph_content.content: In Los Angeles last month, a jury decided in favor of a plaintiff who had claimed that Meta and YouTube hooked her with its addictive products, leading to anxiety and depression. The verdict validated a novel legal theory that social media sites or apps can cause personal injury, similar to cigarettes and opioids. It came straight from the playbook used against Big Tobacco last century.
A day earlier, in New Mexico, a jury found Meta liable for violating state law by failing to protect users from child predators—another novel strategy invoking the state’s consumer-protection laws.

In both cases, social media lost. In the first case, Meta and YouTube combined have to pay $6 million to the plaintiff, a twenty year old woman known as K.M.G. In the New Mexico case, Meta has been ordered to pay $375 million in punitive damages.
The fines themselves mean nothing. But the decisions are bellwether moments—a fork in the road for social media that carries implications for other future technologies (see WILTW April 28, 2025).

For one, the backlash against social media and the push to make the internet safer for kids may finally be gaining legal traction. It’s a case we made three years ago, when we argued that framing Big-Tech accountability through the

page_footer_content.content: 13D RESEARCH & STRATEGY, 13D.COM | PRINT ONCE - DO NOT FORWARD - DO NOT COPY

paragraph_content.content: April 9, 2026
lens of children and families may prove the rare policy weapon that hits people on an emotional level (see WILTW January 12, 2023 and April 3, 2025).
It certainly hit these two juries, nevermind the growing cohort of grassroots initiatives led by parents and communities to loosen technology’s grip on their kids. Will lawmakers follow suit?

In the wake of the verdicts, Senators Marsha Blackburn, Republican of Tennessee, and Richard Blumenthal, Democrat of Connecticut, called for legislators to pass their bill, the Kids Online Safety Act (KOSA), which stalled out in the House last year. If passed, federal momentum would build on laws in more than 30 states banning phones in schools.

The momentum has worldwide reach. In the wake of Australia’s passage in December of a social-media ban for those aged 16 and under, six more democracies are expected to pass similar bans this year, including Spain, Norway, France, Malaysia and Indonesia.

Secondly, assuming they survive the appeals process, these cases will open the floodgates for others to sue social-media companies using the same winning strategy: their products were built with dangerously-habit forming features that have led to an array of serious health problems. Similar individual cases have already been filed around the country, and a massive federal case with more than 2,000 plaintiffs is expected to proceed this summer. Meta and other social-media companies are facing so much pending litigation that keeping track of it all is nearly impossible.
The third reason these cases matter is that they have revealed a crack in Section 230 of the Communications Decency Act, which for thirty years has been the foundation the entire internet rests on.

Section 230 is the law that says these platforms cannot be held liable for their content, or what their users post. Now, with these two cases, juries are saying that it’s not the content that’s the problem. It’s the entire architecture of the platforms themselves.
Technologist and podcaster Casey Newton puts it this way:

page_footer_content.content: 13D RESEARCH & STRATEGY, 13D.COM | PRINT ONCE - DO NOT FORWARD - DO NOT COPY

paragraph_content.content: April 9, 2026

This is not about, oh, “I got harmed by this particular piece of content.” This is about the design of the whole platform. The design feels defective. And the really crazy thing about these cases is that juries agreed with these plaintiffs for the first time. They said, “we like this theory. We think these products are defective.” A few years ago, we weren’t using this language. It was just kind of this more nebulous effect of Instagram on teen girls. Now, we’re adopting this public health framing as a way to discuss the harms of social media. We finally have the right framing.
One legal scholar called the L.A. case in particular a “brick in the wall” that’s been shielding Big Tech.
We quote Catherine M. Sharkey, a professor of law at New York University and an expert on tort and product liability:

The implications of the court decisions [last month] are very, very big. We’re in a new era, a digital era, where we have to rethink definitions for products based on which entities might have superior information to prevent these injuries and accidents.

In the L.A. case, the design features at issue were the filters that can make you look “more beautiful,” along with infinite scroll, autoplay video, the constant barrage of push notifications, and the recommendation-algorithms that power the platform. The prosecution’s central argument was that social media can have consequences as real as those of physical goods, and we should think about it in the same terms. Mark Lanier, a member of K.G.M.’s legal team, described social-media companies as “lions hunting gazelles,” and compared their products to cigarettes, opioids, and ultra-processed foods. But it was K.G.M.’s own account of her experiences that appeared to move members of the jury, some of whom reportedly cried during her testimony.

The New Mexico case was really about child safety. The lawyers were arguing that Instagram in particular has become a playground for predators, especially via their end-to-end encrypted messaging. The thrust of the argument was that Meta has been falsely advertising that these platforms are safe when, in reality, children are being harmed there all the time.

page_footer_content.content: 13D RESEARCH & STRATEGY, 13D.COM | PRINT ONCE - DO NOT FORWARD - DO NOT COPY

paragraph_content.content: April 9, 2026

New Mexico Attorney General Raúl Torrez issued a statement to reporters, writing, “New Mexico is proud to be the first state to hold Meta accountable in court for misleading parents, enabling child exploitation, and harming kids.”

The verdicts pose existential questions for social media companies, AI, and the future of the internet itself. One of the big debates that’s raging in the internet-policy community right now is, can you separate content from design? Some argue that designing a push notification is protected under the First Amendment. But the juries in L.A. and New Mexico called foul, essentially saying that the container is just as dangerous as the stuff inside the container.

More juries are likely to rule the same way. Lawsuits filed by parents are already piling up against AI chatbot companies, alleging that their products are addictive, promote dangerous content, and have directly contributed to the suicide and severe mental-health issues among minors—in short, the same legal strategy that just proved successful against social media companies. In the case of Meta, internal documents have revealed that the platform’s rules explicitly allow their chatbots to have sensual conversations with children, meaning there is hard evidence that Meta knowingly designed a defective and dangerous product.

The L.A. case also raised the issue of monopoly power, with the lawyers arguing—or at least raising the question—that in order to be truly addictive, you have to have scale. Newton summarized it this way: “There’s something about the fact that there’s hundreds of millions of people on Instagram creating content that [generates] that kind of infinite supply of things that you might want to watch that is actually able to addict you.”

And in fact, this is the tack that European regulators have taken: the bigger and richer and more powerful you are, the more responsibility you have to society. In this particular case, Meta has unlimited resources to hire the world’s best cognitive scientists to figure out how to hack users’ brains with the most addictive designs possible. The very spectre of this advantage proved persuasive to jurors.
Newton wrote: “The jury saw that there’s no check on that kind of power right now in U.S. society, except for this litigation.”

page_footer_content.content: 13D RESEARCH & STRATEGY, 13D.COM | PRINT ONCE - DO NOT FORWARD - DO NOT COPY

paragraph_content.content: April 9, 2026

Finally, the arc of smartphones and social media may foretell the future for certain AI technologies. The research firm Gartner called it the “hype cycle,” noting: “First comes tech enthusiasm, then rapid adoption, and finally disenchantment.”

Social media and smartphones are at a later stage of the cycle. Classroom AI use is just gearing up. An illustrative example is Australia. At the very moment it’s implementing a social media ban for young people, it’s also considering an AI-literacy plan, which focuses on embedding AI across educational systems. If there’s a warning embedded in these trials for investors, it’s how quickly tech hype can turn into a full-blown backlash.

title_content.content: The destruction of the U.S. Air Force F-15E Strike Eagle fighter jet last week incinerated 0.05% of the global supply of the critical-mineral rhenium. The war in Iran is proving that U.S. “just-in-time” mineral and defense-technology supply chains are not fit-for-purpose. What are the implications? How to invest.

paragraph_content.content: Rhenium is one of the rarest elements in the earth’s crust—less than 1 part per billion—and one of the smallest metal markets worldwide. It is primarily found in Chilean porphyry-molybdenum deposits that contain 0.001% to 0.2% rhenium. Total global production is only 62 tons per year, excluding approximately 15 tons from scrap and recycling. The total global supply could fit into two pickup trucks. Global supply is mostly from Chile (29 tons), followed by the U.S. and Poland (9.5 tons respectively), and China and Uzbekistan (~5 tons respectively). Rhenium is genuinely rare.
Global rhenium supply is also inherently constrained given that it is only produced as a byproduct of a byproduct. Like most niche, byproduct minor-

page_footer_content.content: 13D RESEARCH & STRATEGY, 13D.COM | PRINT ONCE - DO NOT FORWARD - DO NOT COPY

paragraph_content.content: April 9, 2026

metals markets, increasing supply is tied to the economics of primary metal supply. Given that rhenium does not form its own minerals and exists as a substitute within the molybdenite lattice, its recovery is entirely dependent on the multistage processing of copper-molybdenum concentrates, and it is often lost to waste streams or flue gases. The process of extracting rhenium from coppermolybdenum ores is a complex metallurgical challenge, making supply highly inelastic. The rhenium market is difficult to scale.

Rhenium has recently been classified as a critical mineral by the U.S. Geological Survey (USGS). Its inclusion signals that U.S. policymakers view it as strategically important to economic and national security, and prone to supplychain disruption. Other minerals added to the 2025 USGS list were: copper, potash, boron, lead, metallurgical coal, silicon, silver, and uranium. These minerals were added following the USGS mapping 1,200 trade-disruption scenarios across 84 commodities and ranking them by probability-weighted loss to U.S. GDP from a one-year-long supply disruption.

Rhenium is not substitutable in its main downstream market: jet-engine components. Without rhenium, there are no jet engines. Approximately 80 percent of the global market for rhenium is for nickel super-alloy turbineengine components—particularly the blades for commercial- and militaryjet engines. It has exceptional stability at high temperature, limiting its substitution for performance thresholds in the hottest stages of modern engines. In aerospace, even a small amount of rhenium can materially improve creep resistance and oxidation control, helping engines run hotter and more efficiently. Typical rhenium concentration in nickel-based superalloys is between 3-6%.

Beyond jet engines, approximately 15% of global rhenium demand is for platinum-rhenium catalysts for petroleum reforming. Rhenium demand in 2025 was boosted by the U.S. administration’s pro-oil stance and cuts to renewable-energy tax-credits. Rhenium-based alloys are also increasingly used as alternatives in medical implants.

Ultimately, the global-rhenium supply chain is yet another clear example that the “just-in-time” defense-procurement supply chain is no longer fit for purpose. The unvarnished truth is that Western-defense procurement relies on small, unscalable, and highly-concentrated metals markets.

page_footer_content.content: 13D RESEARCH & STRATEGY, 13D.COM | PRINT ONCE - DO NOT FORWARD - DO NOT COPY

paragraph_content.content: April 9, 2026

item_content.content: The U.S. Air Force F-15E Strike Eagle fighter jet shot down over Iran on Friday last week contained approximately 0.032 tons of rhenium—0.05% of total annual global supply. The F-15E’s Pratt & Whitney F100-series engines, particularly the advanced 229 variant used in F-15 Eagle jets, use significant quantities of rhenium in their highpressure turbine sections. These high-pressure turbine (HPT) blades are manufactured from the single-crystal superalloy PW1484, which typically contains approximately 3% rhenium to enhance thermal stability and creep resistance. Exact individual blade masses are proprietary to the company, although industry benchmarks for F100-series engines indicate a total rhenium loading of approximately 16.30 kg per engine, which equates to 32.60 kg for a twin-engine F-15 aircraft.

Defense aviation is manifestly the leading sector vulnerable to rhenium supply-chain disruption. Commercial aviation is equally vulnerable. Boeing has forecast demand of nearly 44,000 new airplanes over the next 20 years. This increase in engine production will force significant downward pressure on the rhenium supply chain, potentially leading to shortages. Where supply cannot meet demand, commercial engine makers will face longer lead times and higher inventory costs. In Western-defense-aviation supply chains, this creates significant consequences for fleet availability and defense readiness.

Rhenium is the classic bottleneck minor-metal market. However, unlike many other minor-metals markets we have written about, global rhenium supply is not highly concentrated in China; nor is it particularly vulnerable to potential Chinese-trade disruptions. Global supply is only threatened if a copper mine, concentrator or smelter is disrupted.

Despite this, rhenium supply is still poised to become a key geopoliticalmineral battleground of the future. Today, China is the largest global importer of rhenium. Despite current volatile jet-fuel prices, commercial aviation demand in China is entering a significant structural-growth phase. China ramped up demand in 2025, forcing upward pressure on rhenium prices over the year.
 One of the few areas the U.S. has leveraged in its ongoing trade negotiations with China is its jet-engine exports. Last year, the

page_footer_content.content: 13D RESEARCH & STRATEGY, 13D.COM | PRINT ONCE - DO NOT FORWARD - DO NOT COPY

paragraph_content.content: April 9, 2026

Commercial Aircraft Corporation of China, Ltd. (COMAC), the largest stateowned-aircraft manufacturer in China, drastically revised the short-term delivery outlook of its flagship C919 aircraft due to the U.S. export ban of key engine parts. The Aviation Industry Corporation of China (AVIC), China’s stateowned aerospace and defense conglomerate and the primary manufacturer of China’s domestic-military aircraft, is also a key supplier of parts for COMAC. China’s three-largest commercial airlines are also majority state-owned.

item_content.content: The rhenium narrative symbolizes a wider breakdown in modern Western-defense-procurement supply chains. As we are witnessing in the Middle East, modern conflict is punishing these slow, outdated supply chains. Modern warfare is increasingly shaped by inexpensive drones and low-cost, high-return operations capable of inflicting significant equipment losses. In the first week of the war in Iran, a single Iranian drone (costing approximately $30,000) damaged an AN/FPS-132 phased-array early warning radar in Qatar. The AN/FPF-132 took eight years to build and cost $1.1 billion. The “just-in-time” procurement paradigm, whether for mining the upstream critical minerals themselves, or for the finished components supplied by subtier manufacturers, is clearly no longer effectively serving this kind of warfare (see WILTW report).

Long-term strategic planning could address some of these endemic supply-chain vulnerabilities. However, the Trump administration’s downgrading of long-term strategic institutions—such as abolishing the Department of War’s Office of Net Assessment (ONA), cutting 50% of Office of Director of National Intelligence (ODNI) staffing, defunding the State Department’s Global Engagement Center, and closing nearly 30 U.S. consulates—signals the beginnings of an erosion of U.S.-strategic foresight that will in time undermine the development of critical national industries and their supply chains.

1 Rhenium is therefore more than a niche industrial input: it is part of the resilience of an entire system. Rhenium is a single-point-of-failure in a wider matrix of other single-points-of-failure. The real cost of the Iran war is therefore not in dollars, or even materiel, but the weeks or months it will take to repair and replace U.S.-defense supplies. Time is the real cost. Building institutional capacity on this scale—repairing and restructuring the supply chain to meet the new demands of modern warfare and address the specific strategic-material chokepoints—will take the U.S. years it likely doesn’t have.

title_content.content: Electrotech —solar, wind, batteries, electric vehicles, and heat pumps—offer an affordable escape from the volatile world of fossil energy.

paragraph_content.content: The war in Iran has triggered the second energy crisis in four years and may be the most significant shock to the global economy since the GFC. Over 40 major energy infrastructure assets have been damaged so far. Even if attacks cease today, the damage is so extensive that it will take years to repair.

Jet fuel prices have increased by 70%, gasoline prices by 30% to 40%, and wholesale gas by at least 60%. Heating oil, which many rural households rely on, has climbed even higher. Fossil-fuel volatility has become a clear threat to global stability.
About 75% of the world's population lives in fossil-fuel-importing countries.
These countries spent at least $1.7 trillion in 2024 on oil and gas imports, at an average oil price of $80 per barrel.

For every $10-per-barrel increase in oil prices, global net import costs rise by about $160 billion a year, according to a new report by Ember, a London-based clean-energy research group. If oil averages $110 a barrel this year, it will cost importing nations an additional $480 billion, compared to 2024.
Before the war, most forecasts had projected oil prices of $60 to $65 a barrel for 2026.

page_footer_content.content: 13D RESEARCH & STRATEGY, 13D.COM | PRINT ONCE - DO NOT FORWARD - DO NOT COPY

paragraph_content.content: April 9, 2026

LNG importers face a similar challenge. For every $1-per-million-Britishthermal-units (MMBtu) increase in LNG prices, global net import costs rise by about $20 billion per year. Goldman Sachs forecasts a $12 to $14 MMBtu price increase for European and Asian gas imports through Q2.

For importers of oil and gas, the fastest and most affordable way to protect themselves from the price and supply volatility of oil and gas imports is with electrotech—solar and wind power generation, battery storage, electric vehicles, and heat pumps, as we have previously noted. (See reports.)
Electrotech technologies could replace up to 70% of current fossil fuel imports, according to the Ember report.
Fortunately, electrotech alternatives have become cheaper, better, and more readily available since the 2022 energy crisis.

For example, since 2022, the cost of solar panels has dropped by half, and annual solar installations have nearly tripled. Battery prices decreased by 36%, and yearly deployment of grid batteries increased sevenfold.

Unlike fossil fuels, wind and solar energy resources are abundant and accessible everywhere. The sun and wind are not affected by geopolitics, tariffs, shipping delays, chokepoints, price volatility, currency fluctuations, or conflicts.
Although intermittent, solar and wind power have a significant efficiency advantage since 60% to 65% of energy produced by fossil fuels is lost as waste heat.
The capital costs of building solar and wind installations are now similar to those of a new gas-power plant with comparable power output.

paragraph_content.content: China’s solar panels have become the cheapest source of electricity in history, with a panel costing the same as a sheet of plywood, as we have noted. A solar panel or wind turbine can generate electricity for 3 decades with no fuel costs, no price increases, and no supply risk. (See WILTW January 8, 2026.)

That’s why the world installed a record 814 gigawatts (GW) of new solar and wind capacity in 2025, 17% more than in 2024 (696 GW). That’s roughly double South America’s total power-generation capacity from all sources.

The addition of 814 GW of solar and wind last year was enough to displace 15% of global gas generation, and reduce annual gas import costs by roughly $138 billion at current market prices, according to a new report by Ember.

For example, a group of 74 low-income countries spent $155 billion on net fossil fuel imports in 2024—a significant annual drain on public finances and foreign exchange reserves. Those import costs may increase by 40% or more this year. Countries will likely be forced to moderate domestic-fuel price increases through bigger fuel subsidies, pushing budgets further into deficit.

Many of these countries, such as Pakistan, have at least 3 times the volume of solar imports, mostly for rooftop solar, than their official data shows, according to another new report from Ember.

We quote Sara Jane Ahmed, Managing Director and Finance Advisor of the 74-nation coalition:
The old trade-off between climate and development is over. Vulnerable countries are leading a rapid clean energy shift, leapfrogging fossil fuels and even richer nations.

For example, Cuba, currently under an oil embargo by the U.S., increased its solar capacity by 350% since 2024, thanks to low-cost Chinese solar panels. Solar and other renewables may provide 15% of Cuba’s power this year, a vital lifeline for a country experiencing widespread blackouts due to the embargo. Cuba plans to nearly double solar capacity by 2028.

Asian countries with coal reserves will replace some gas imports with coal. However, it is likely to be a short-term substitution. Low-cost solar panels and today’s much lower-cost batteries are price-competitive with existing coal in many markets. Moreover, many countries, including Thailand, the Philippines, South Korea, and India, have removed many permitting requirements to speed the installation of renewables.

For example, solar and batteries can already meet 90% of India’s electricity demand at a price competitive with existing coal, according to a new report. Equally important, solar and batteries can provide that power without the billions of dollars in health impacts, lost productivity, and crop losses from coal’s air pollution.

Over 500 million people still lack access to electricity. Another 500 million face unreliable supply. Low-cost, off-grid solar and electrification can finally bring reliable modern energy to these people. Fossil energy is no longer essential to economic development, the Ember report concludes.

Electric 2- and 3-wheel vehicles, along with electric cars and trucks, cut oil consumption by 2.3 million barrels per day last year. That will jump to 5.25 billion bbl/day in 2030, according to BloombergNEF's analysis.

Electric vehicles have become the preferred new vehicle to buy in more and more countries, as we recently noted. (See WILTW February 5, 2026.) Electric cars and trucks made up over 10% of new sales in 39 countries in 2025.

title_content.content: Thanks to its fleet of EVs, China saves over $28 billion a year in avoided oil imports with an oil price of $80 a barrel. The EU saves $8 billion a year.

paragraph_content.content: The rise in EV sales is mainly due to increased cost competitiveness. An EV is also cheaper to operate, saving up to $2,200 a year in fuel costs, according to a 2024 analysis by the U.S. Department of Transportation.
And all this is before the war in Iran.
With U.S. gasoline prices 30% to 40% higher, even EV-shy Americans are giving EVs another look.
U.S. used-EV sales increased 12% in Q1 compared to last year. Meanwhile, new EV sales dropped 28% due to higher-priced models and the Trump Administration's discontinuation of the $7,500 tax credit.

However, in much of the world, EV sales are soaring amid higher gasoline and diesel prices. In Q1, India's EV sales jumped 24.6%, Europe was up 31%, and the UK was up 24.2%. South Korea saw a 172% increase in February thanks to a revised subsidy program. In March, Germany's EV sales jumped 66% year-onyear, while Australia saw a huge 89% increase.

title_content.content: The U.S. is actively undermining its relationships with allies. By redirecting Swiss funds without authorization, Washington is encouraging other countries to reconsider their commercial and financial relationships. The implications for global capital are immense.

paragraph_content.content: According to Werner Salzmann, a senator from the country’s largest party, the Swiss People’s Party: “It’s frustrating when we impose a payment freeze and the money is simply redirected…The [U.S. government’s Foreign Military Sales (FMS) Program] contracts are producing more and more interpretation problems. We need to think carefully about whether we want to keep signing such agreements.”

A few days ago, Switzerland announced that it had in fact halted payments last autumn for the U.S. Patriot missile system given long delays. However, without asking for permission, the U.S. circumvented this payment freeze by redirecting Swiss funds earmarked for the F-35 fighter jet to the Patriot system.

Switzerland was meant to receive the Patriot system between this year and 2028 but more pressing needs in Ukraine delayed this by five years. Due to the war in the Middle East, delivery could be delayed even further and the cost to Switzerland for procuring this system may rise by at least 50%.
Recently, Poland reportedly turned down a request by the U.S. to send its Patriots to the Middle East.

According to the Minister of Defence Martin Pfister: “We’ve informed them [the Swiss Federal Council] about this [Patriot termination] option today and we’re also making this option public today…We are still assuming today that we will receive these systems; we don’t know when, and we are currently in the process of negotiating all possible options with the U.S.…This also includes a complete termination.”

page_footer_content.content: 13D RESEARCH & STRATEGY, 13D.COM | PRINT ONCE - DO NOT FORWARD - DO NOT COPY

paragraph_content.content: April 9, 2026

Switzerland’s Department of Defence, Civil Protection and Sport (DDPS) said that the U.S.’s “reprioritization” of deliveries “significantly alters the contractual basis” of the acquisition. The nation’s Federal Council also added that these changes in U.S. priorities have “profoundly altered the contractual basis.”
In March, Switzerland decided to reduce its F-35 order by six aircraft, after a contract dispute over price increases with the U.S. government.

According to the Swiss government, as a result of the U.S. redirecting funds, if the liquidity of the U.S.’s Swiss FMS fund falls below a “critical threshold, projects can be suspended and, if it falls further, terminated…This could affect not only the Patriot procurement but the entire Swiss Foreign Military Sales (FMS) portfolio with the USA.”

Just recently Swiss government released a statement: “The export of war materiel to countries involved in the international armed conflict with Iran cannot be authorised for the duration of the conflict.” Furthermore, a number of European counties have blocked airspace to the U.S. military.
The ideological rift between the U.S. and Europe continues to widen.
In a sharp rebuke of Trump’s actions, the European Council President António Cost says: “Any targeting of civilian infrastructure, namely energy facilities, is illegal and unacceptable.”
In an interview with the Telegraph last week, Trump said that he is seriously considering pulling the U.S. out of NATO, with the matter “beyond reconsideration.”
The far-right in Europe is also sharply rebuking Trump. The red line for them was the threat to invade Greenland. (See WILTW January 22, 2026.)

As Sudha David-Wilp, a vice president of the German Marshall Fund policy institute, told Washington Post: “For them, sovereignty and national borders are very important. And, so for the United States to say it can just come in and change ownership of territory, that to them is probably anathema and undermines their whole view on the international system.”

page_footer_content.content: 13D RESEARCH & STRATEGY, 13D.COM | PRINT ONCE - DO NOT FORWARD - DO NOT COPY

paragraph_content.content: April 9, 2026

In an interview with France Inter radio, Marine Le Pen of the National Rally party, the most popular party in France, pointed to the “catastrophic consequence” of the war in Iran, calling it a “mistake” and criticised Trump’s “erratic war goals.” Speaking to Le Parisien she said: “Donald Trump clearly did not fully appreciate the impact of his intervention [in Iran]. In fact, it is becoming apparent that very little preparation was done…We therefore feel that these strikes were carried out blindly.”

Germany’s most popular party, the AfD party, has asked its MPs to scale back visits to the U.S. for the purposes of building ties with the MAGA movement. According to a party official: “Even before the war it was suboptimal…The AfD wants to keep contact more targeted and will no longer send large groups.”
Alice Weidel, who alongside Tino Chrupalla, is the co-leader of the AfD, called Trump’s attack on Iran “a catastrophe.”
Chrupalla has said that it is time to remove allied troops and nuclear weapons from Germany in order to pursue an “independent” foreign policy.

At the start of the year, we noted that in just the past three years,  European investors increased their holdings of U.S. assets by 91%, equivalent to $4.9 trillion. Furthermore, half of the $10.4 trillion in U.S. stocks held by Europe is owned by the eight countries that Trump has threatened with tariffs. (See WILTW January 29, 2026.)
In context of all of the above, it begs the question, how long can capital keep flowing from Europe to the U.S.? Is a reversal in the cards?
In February of this year, before the disruption caused by war in the Middle East, European equity markets experienced their largest ever recorded monthly inflow of capital.
As Jean Monnet, the Founding Father of the EU, said: “Europe will be forged in crises, and will be the sum of the solutions adopted for those crises.”

paragraph_content.content: April 9, 2026

title_content.content: Could Europe use this crisis to rejuvenate the single market and attract capital?

paragraph_content.content: The current crisis gives Europe added impetus to strengthen the single market and carry out reforms to make the economy more competitive. As we see in the following chart, as of the end of last year, the majority of Draghi’s proposals were still in progress.
