paragraph_content.content: As we wrote in WILTW November 20, 2025, creating a “military Schengen” and strengthening military capability is a way to revive the single market. A consequence of this is capital being repatriated back to Europe. (See WILTW January 8, 2026.)

Last summer, Jeff Currie wrote a paper entitled “The New Martial Plan” in which he noted the following: “Europe could spend an additional €4-5 trillion on defence over the next decade based on current goals, rising to €9 trillion if adjacent infrastructure is included, and at the limit could approach €14 trillion if Europe matched what the US spent on average over the past 60 years and what Poland is spending now.”
Europe has the fiscal space to do this. The debt-to-GDP ratio in the European Union is 82.1% and the continent is a triple-A rated entity, an increasing rarity.

title_content.content: By strengthening its defence industry, Europe can use this opportunity to internationalise the use of the euro and attract capital.

paragraph_content.content: Last year, during the tariff crisis, Christine Lagarde said that the ongoing changes create the opening for a “global euro moment.” Furthermore, she said that increasing the international role of the euro rests upon creating a geopolitical foundation and hard power. (See WILTW May 29, 2025.) This is now more important than ever given the geopolitical backdrop today.

Furthermore, issuing pan-European bonds to strengthen the defence industry ensures a deep supply of bonds. As we wrote in WILTW June 19, 2025, Europe is exploring proposals to create a deep, liquid Eurobond market as an alternative to the UST market.

paragraph_content.content: April 9, 2026

title_content.content: “Friendship is unnecessary, like philosophy, like art,” C.S. Lewis wrote in The Four Loves, his 1960 theological book that examines the meaning of love. “It has no survival value; rather it is one of those things that gives value to survival.”

paragraph_content.content: We’ve been thinking a lot about this quote recently as we try to understand more deeply the “Friendship Recession” that has gripped American life over the last several decades.

We’ve previously written about how this crisis has hit men particularly hard (see WILTW March 13, 2025). But in truth, across all generations and all sexes, Americans are spending less time with each other than at any point in modern history: We don’t hang out as much, we don’t volunteer as much, we don’t participate in community groups as much—at a cost that has imperiled our mental health and happiness.

The aftershocks of America’s decline in friendships are especially detrimental to young people. To learn how to navigate the world, kids and teens need to be in the world. As research has shown, the best kind of play is unsupervised and outdoors (see WILTW March 5, 2026). But equally important, it’s with other kids. Negotiating boundaries, caring for another’s needs, managing conflict, and tolerating discomfort—the lessons we learn from our friendships at a young age greatly influence the quality of our relationships later in life.

“Friendships during the teenage years provide youth with one of their first forays into intimate consensual relationships,” reports Dr. David Szwedo of James Madison University, co-author of a 2024 paper that tracked 184 participants, from their early teen years into their late twenties. Over time, researchers found, close friendships during the older teen years in particular, predicted lower social anxiety and romantic insecurity and higher job satisfaction. “Because friends may come and go, friendships are a context in which teens

page_footer_content.content: 13D RESEARCH & STRATEGY, 13D.COM | PRINT ONCE - DO NOT FORWARD - DO NOT COPY

paragraph_content.content: April 9, 2026
must develop skills to maintain and grow the friendship or risk its loss. These skills are likely to be subsequently helpful for forming future friendships and longer-term romantic relationships.”

As we’ve written about extensively in recent years, rates of loneliness have soared as technology—led in no small part by the advent of the smartphone in 2007—has increasingly dominated how we live, work, and socialize (see articles). The cost of this shift is enormous: Loneliness makes people more prone to depression, dementia progression, hypertension, stroke, and cancer.

But Big Tech isn’t the sole culprit of this development. The dominance of a screen-based life and the rise of social media merely accelerated a social isolation trend that began long before the first personal computer hit the market. In his seminal 2000 book,  Bowling Alone: The Collapse and Revival of American Community, political scientist Robert D. Putnam examined this change.

Putnam argues that the 1970s marked a new era of withdrawal in American life as the shared interest and investment in social infrastructure—our community centers, our libraries, our public pools—waned. Involvement in a range of organizations, from churches to book clubs to bowling leagues, eroded. From 1985 to 1994 alone, participation in these kinds of community endeavors fell by nearly half.

In retreating from community, Americans have also retreated from each other. One landmark study found that the percentage of U.S. adults who report having zero close friends has quadrupled since 1990 to 12%, while the number of those who report having ten or more close friends has fallen to one-third of its previous level.

The writer and podcaster Derek Thompson describes this movement as the “privatization of leisure.” He argues that “America is suffering a kind of ritual recession, with fewer community-based routines and more entertainment for, and empowerment of, individuals and the aloneness that they choose.”

The data on young people is especially telling. In 1996, American high school sophomores went out with friends an average of 2.5 times per week, according to the long-running Monitoring the Future project at the University of Michigan. A decade later, at the very time when AOL Instant Messenger had gone mainstream, that same age bracket was only hanging out in person 2.3 times per week. By 2020—just before the pandemic roiled social life— the rate had collapsed to 1.5 times per week.

paragraph_content.content: April 9, 2026

All manner of teen life has paid the price: Young Americans are less likely than previous generations to get their driver’s license, to go on a date, to get a job. In the late 1970s, 77 percent of high-school seniors had part-time work during the school year; by the mid-2010s, only 55 percent did.

One of the leading experts in this field of study is Dr. Joseph Allen, a professor of psychology at the University of Virginia. Since 1998, he and his team have overseen a longitudinal study of nearly 200 participants who were 13 years old at the time of the project’s launch.

In an important 2021 survey Allen’s team asked study participants, who were at the time 27 years old, questions about the quality of their adult social and romantic relationships, as well as about their depressive symptoms and work performance. Next, they compared the data to earlier interviews with the cohort, their parents, and teen friends on relationship quality. Good teen friendships, the team found, fostered success later in life.

“Quality relationships with teens were a far better predictor of all of these outcomes than was the quality of the teens’ relationships with their parents,” Allen reported. “The flip side is just as you’d expect, which is if you did not have good, quality, best-friend relationships as a teenager, in adulthood, by age 25, 26 and 27, you had worse work performance, you had lower-quality relationships with other adults, you had poor-quality romantic relationships and you were more likely to be depressed.”

All of this comes at an interesting point in human history. Despite all of our aloneness, American life has never felt so crowded. Our days are consumed by communication: texting, posting, DMing, emailing, calling. This new order of relationship maintenance is unending and exhausting.
Has modern life left us too tired to do the hard work of forming real friendships?

Nurturing real bonds with others requires time and patience. It means navigating discomforts, permitting vulnerabilities, and allowing for self-reflection and even criticism. Our increasingly-dominant online life not only consumes time for real-world relationships but also disincentivizes our pursuit of them.
“Social rejection doesn’t just hurt emotionally—it activates the amygdala, the brain’s threat detection center, and triggers the same neural pathways as

page_footer_content.content: 13D RESEARCH & STRATEGY, 13D.COM | PRINT ONCE - DO NOT FORWARD - DO NOT COPY

paragraph_content.content: April 9, 2026

physical pain,” writes Carolyn Bruckmann in the February 2025 issue of Harvard Kennedy School Magazine. “Over time, this makes socializing feel riskier, and to avoid that discomfort, we increasingly turn to psychological shortcuts. Digital interactions offer a sense of control: behind our screens, we can curate our self-presentation, sidestep awkward social cues, and get quick dopamine hits that create the illusion of connection. Given the way our brains are wired, we also gravitate toward the safest, most familiar spaces—choosing the comfort of home and close family over the unpredictability of community spaces and new relationships.”

Still, humans are inherently social animals. It is in our lifeblood to make and build connections. To feel connected. And as we’ve noted previously, there is a growing hunger from younger generations to forge a healthy life outside of tech’s domain (see WILTW May 22, 2025; February 12, 2026). Across the country, several innovative programs have emerged to help young Americans overcome the country’s friendship recession.
Here are a few that have caught our attention:

item_content.content: ■ Project for Public Spaces (PPS): Working with different local organizations, PPS transforms “dead” urban spaces into community gathering spots. In San Antonio, Texas, for example, it helped turn a concrete underpass into a dedicated teen hangout, complete with a shaded basketball court, ping pong tables, large picnic tables, and seating areas that encourage “loitering.”

1 Log Off Movement: Launched in 2020 by a high school senior in response to her own struggles with social media, Log Off provides a toolkit for teens to build analog networks in their own towns. Its popular “Forks Up, Phones Down” campaign encourages teens to dine together to build authentic human connection.

 CoGenerate: This national program matches teens with seniors to work together on projects that address different issues affecting their respective communities, from climate action to healthcare access.

paragraph_content.content: These programs are an inspiration and we hope in the coming years to see others like them develop. The vitality of our survival depends on it.
